era_context: The early 18th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires. The Qing Dynasty under the Yongzheng Emperor in China, a period of administrative reform and centralisation. The Ottoman Empire in the Tulip Period (1718–1730), a relatively peaceful and culturally flourishing era. The Russian Empire under Peter II (r. 1727–1730), a period of political instability. The War of Polish Succession (1733–1735) approaching.

events.title: Bering Expedition Departs from Kamchatka
events.description: Vitus Bering, a Danish explorer in Russian service, departed from Kamchatka in 1728 to explore the northeastern coast of Asia and determine whether Asia and North America were connected by land. The expedition sailed north through the strait that would later bear Bering's name (the Bering Strait). Although Bering did not sight North America during this first expedition, he confirmed that Asia and North America were separated by water. The expedition returned to Kamchatka in 1729. This was the first of Bering's two major expeditions; the second (1733–1741) would be even more significant. The 1728 expedition was crucial in establishing Russian claims to the Far East and in advancing geographical knowledge.
events.certainty_note: Bering's first expedition is well-documented in Russian records and Bering's own accounts. The departure date of 1728 is precisely established.

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor's Administrative Reforms Continue
events.description: The Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1722–1735) continued his intensive administrative reforms and centralisation of power in 1728. His anti-corruption campaigns were ongoing, and he was systematically eliminating rivals and consolidating imperial authority. Yongzheng's reforms included the expansion of the imperial bureaucracy, the centralisation of tax collection, and the elimination of hereditary offices. These reforms were highly effective in strengthening the Qing state and would be continued and expanded under his successor, the Qianlong Emperor. In 1728, these reforms were at their height.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty

events.certainty_note: Yongzheng's administrative reforms are well-documented in Chinese imperial records.

events.title: Peter II's Continued Reign in Russia
events.description: Peter II (1715–1730) continued his reign as Tsar of Russia in 1728. His reign (1727–1730) was marked by political instability and the reversal of many of Peter the Great's policies. The nobility had gained significant power at the expense of the crown, and the young tsar was dominated by court factions. In 1728, Peter II was only 13 years old and had little real power. His reign would end with his death in 1730, creating a succession crisis. The year 1728 represents a period of Russian political weakness and internal division.
events.certainty_note: Peter II's reign dates are precisely documented in Russian imperial records.

events.title: Tulip Period Flourishing in Ottoman Empire
events.description: The Tulip Period (Lale Devri, 1718–1730) was at its height in 1728. This era was marked by relative peace, cultural flourishing, and the introduction of European architectural and artistic styles into Ottoman culture. Grand vizier Ibrahim Pasha was the dominant political figure, and his patronage of the arts and architecture was extensive. The period represented a moment of Ottoman cultural confidence and openness to European influences. In 1728, the Tulip Period was approaching its end, which would occur in 1730 with political upheaval.

sources.name: European travel accounts and diplomatic reports

events.certainty_note: The Tulip Period is well-documented in Ottoman and European sources. 1728 falls securely within this era.

events.title: Continued European Colonial Expansion and Slave Trade
events.description: In 1728, European colonial powers were actively consolidating their control over colonial territories and intensifying the slave trade. Millions of Africans were being forcibly transported to the Americas to work on plantations. The triangular trade (Europe-Africa-Americas) was becoming increasingly profitable and systematic. Indigenous populations in the Americas were being displaced, enslaved, or decimated by disease. The year 1728 represents a point of acceleration in European colonial dominance and the intensification of the slave trade.

sources.name: European colonial records and trading company archives
Historiographic consensus on colonial expansion and the slave trade

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of European colonial expansion and slave trade intensification in 1728 is well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. However, Bering's first expedition is sometimes overstated in significance; it did not immediately lead to major geographical discoveries or territorial claims. The expedition's primary value was in confirming the separation of Asia and North America, which was already suspected by many geographers.
historiographic_note: 1728 is moderately well-documented in European and East Asian sources, particularly regarding political, diplomatic, and exploratory events. The historical record is heavily weighted toward court politics, diplomacy, military affairs, and European exploration. Economic, social, and cultural developments are less precisely dated. The year is significant primarily as a point within larger processes (Yongzheng's consolidation, the Tulip Period, European colonial expansion, Bering's exploration). African, indigenous American, and Oceanic histories are largely absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: Bering's first expedition (1728–1729) led to his second, more famous expedition (1733–1741).
Yongzheng's administrative reforms set the stage for Qianlong's even more successful reign.
